question_patterns: Tell me about a time you had to make a difficult decision with incomplete data.
Describe a situation where you disagreed with your manager — what did you do?
Give an example of when you went above and beyond for a customer.
Tell me about a time you failed. What did you learn?
Describe the most complex technical problem you've solved.
Amazon.culture_notes: Expect heavy LP-based behavioural questions. Every answer must map to at least one of Amazon's 16 Leadership Principles. Have 6-8 STAR stories ready that can flex to any LP.
avoid_topics: Generic teamwork stories with no personal ownership
Answers that credit the team without 'I'

question_patterns: Design a URL shortening service that handles 1 billion requests/day.
Describe a time you had to influence without authority.
Walk me through how you'd improve Google Maps' ETA accuracy.
Tell me about a time you handled ambiguity in a project.
How would you design YouTube's recommendation engine?
Google.culture_notes: Google assesses four dimensions: GCA, leadership, role knowledge, and Googleyness (comfort with ambiguity, collaboration, intellectual humility). System design rounds are critical for L4+.
avoid_topics: Overconfident answers claiming sole credit
Rigid thinking in system design

question_patterns: Tell me about the most impactful project you've worked on.
How do you decide what to work on when everything feels urgent?
Design Facebook's News Feed ranking system.
Describe a time you had to make a trade-off between speed and quality.
Walk me through a time you used data to change a product direction.
Meta.culture_notes: Meta is impact-obsessed. Every story should quantify impact. They look for people who move fast with high agency. Behavioural prep should emphasise personal impact, cross-functional work, and shipping velocity.
avoid_topics: Vague process stories with no measurable outcomes
Slow-moving consensus-focused anecdotes

question_patterns: Tell me about a time you had to learn something completely new under pressure.
How do you handle feedback that challenges your core assumptions?
Describe a situation where you made a product more accessible or inclusive.
Walk me through designing an Azure service at global scale.
Tell me about a time you helped a struggling teammate.
Microsoft.culture_notes: Microsoft deeply values growth mindset — show how you learn from failure, seek feedback, and evolve. Culture interviews probe empathy and inclusion. Technical rounds often involve Azure cloud scenarios.
avoid_topics: Fixed mindset answers ('I already knew this')
Individual glory without team collaboration

question_patterns: Walk me through a project where you had to obsess over quality despite time pressure.
Tell me about the hardest technical problem you've ever debugged.
How do you approach designing for billions of users with diverse needs?
Describe a time you pushed back on requirements you thought were wrong.
What's the last product you used where you noticed an exceptional detail in the UX?
Apple.culture_notes: Apple expects extreme ownership and quality obsession. Interviews are often deep technical dives. They probe intuition for user experience and product craft. Secrecy means candidates rarely get feedback.
avoid_topics: Shipping fast at the cost of quality
Generic high-level answers lacking technical depth

question_patterns: Tell me about a time you gave or received brutally honest feedback.
Describe a decision you made with full ownership and no manager approval.
How would you design Netflix's content recommendation system?
Tell me about a time you set a context for a team rather than prescribing a solution.
Walk me through your approach to handling a critical production incident.
Netflix.culture_notes: Netflix hires 'stunning colleagues' — high performers who thrive with autonomy. The Keeper Test question ('Would your manager fight to keep you?') is implicit. They prize candor, self-sufficiency, and mature judgment.
avoid_topics: Needing lots of hand-holding
Consensus-seeking without a clear owner

question_patterns: How would you design a payments API that handles idempotency at scale?
Tell me about a time you simplified a complex system for end users.
Walk me through your understanding of distributed transaction consistency.
Describe how you'd approach rate limiting in a multi-tenant API.
Tell me about a technical decision you made that you later changed your mind on.
Stripe.culture_notes: Stripe is intensely intellectual and writing-heavy. They value curiosity and the ability to engage seriously with complex problems. API design sensibility is paramount. Written take-homes are common.
avoid_topics: Surface-level API knowledge
Answers that skip edge cases

question_patterns: How would you design Uber's surge pricing system?
Walk me through matching riders and drivers at 10M concurrent users.
Tell me about a time you delivered a project with extremely tight deadlines.
How would you detect and prevent fraudulent rides in real time?
Describe a time you had to influence product direction without direct authority.
Uber.culture_notes: Uber interviews heavily test real-time system design and marketplace thinking. Technical rounds often use ride-sharing scenarios. They look for scrappy, high-agency individuals who move fast.
avoid_topics: Slow consensus building
Theoretical answers without operational pragmatism

question_patterns: Tell me about a time you built something that created real community or belonging.
How would you redesign Airbnb's review system to build more host-guest trust?
Describe a time you navigated a complex trust or safety tradeoff.
Walk me through designing search ranking for a two-sided marketplace.
Tell me about a time your product thinking was challenged by user research.
Airbnb.culture_notes: Airbnb's mission ('Belong Anywhere') permeates every interview. They look for genuine empathy for both hosts and guests. The 'core values' interview is a significant filter — prepare stories aligning with community, belonging, and trust.
avoid_topics: Pure optimisation answers ignoring human trust
Answers with no community or empathy angle

rubric_focus: Free speech and content moderation tradeoffs
question_patterns: Design Twitter's home timeline at 500M users.
How would you approach content moderation decisions at scale?
Walk me through the fanout-on-read vs fanout-on-write tradeoff for feeds.
How would you detect trending topics in real time?
Describe a time you built or operated a high-throughput event pipeline.
Twitter / X.culture_notes: Twitter/X interviews heavily probe real-time distributed systems knowledge — the timeline, notifications, and trends systems are canonical case studies. Content policy tradeoffs are common product-sense questions.
avoid_topics: Ignoring scale constraints
Naive feed implementation without considering celebrity accounts (hotspot problem)

question_patterns: How would you improve LinkedIn's connection recommendation algorithm?
Tell me about a time you built a product that helped people grow professionally.
Design a system to detect fake profiles at LinkedIn's scale.
How would you measure the success of LinkedIn's Jobs feature?
Walk me through a time you used data to prioritise a product roadmap.
LinkedIn.culture_notes: LinkedIn interviews emphasise network effects, professional empathy, and graph-based thinking. Product questions often centre on the professional ecosystem. Responsible AI and fairness are growing focus areas.
avoid_topics: Consumer social thinking without professional context
Privacy-invasive solutions for network growth

question_patterns: Tell me about a time you went above and beyond to ensure customer success.
Describe how you'd design a multi-tenant SaaS database at Salesforce scale.
How do you handle a situation where company values conflict with a business goal?
Walk me through a time you used data to directly improve a customer's outcome.
Tell me about your experience with CRM systems or enterprise SaaS platforms.
Salesforce.culture_notes: Salesforce's Ohana (family) culture is central. They screen hard for customer obsession and values alignment. The V2MOM framework shapes how teams think. Multi-tenancy architecture knowledge is critical for technical roles.
avoid_topics: Ignoring customer impact in technical decisions
Individual glory at team expense

question_patterns: Walk me through how you'd price an options contract using Black-Scholes.
Tell me about a time you identified and mitigated a significant risk.
How would you design a low-latency trading system?
Describe the biggest trade-off you've made between speed and accuracy.
What do you think drives current volatility in equity markets?
Goldman Sachs.culture_notes: Goldman screens for both technical depth (quant/engineering roles) and market fluency. Integrity and client-first thinking are table stakes. Expect numerical estimation, market commentary, and problem-solving under pressure.
avoid_topics: Lack of market awareness
Ethically ambiguous answers to integrity scenarios

question_patterns: How would you redesign Adobe's onboarding for new Photoshop users?
Tell me about a time you made a complex technical tool more accessible.
Design a collaborative real-time editing system at Adobe's scale.
Describe a time you balanced creative freedom with technical constraints.
How would you measure the success of a new Creative Cloud feature?
Adobe.culture_notes: Adobe values creativity balanced with engineering rigour. The shift from perpetual to subscription (Creative Cloud) informs many product questions. Design sensibility matters even for engineers.
avoid_topics: Pure optimisation with no UX awareness
Lack of creative tool context

question_patterns: How would you design Spotify's Discover Weekly recommendation system?
Walk me through streaming audio to 500M users with low latency.
Tell me about a time you worked in a self-organising team structure.
How would you measure the health of Spotify's creator ecosystem?
Design Spotify's podcast analytics pipeline.
Spotify.culture_notes: Spotify's autonomous Squad/Tribe model means they prize team ownership and self-direction. Music and audio domain knowledge impresses. Data and ML thinking is embedded in product and engineering roles.
avoid_topics: Hierarchical command-and-control thinking
Ignoring content creator needs in product design

question_patterns: How would you approach designing a self-driving decision system for edge cases?
Tell me about a time you challenged an existing assumption and found a better solution.
How would you architect the over-the-air update system for Tesla vehicles?
Describe a time you shipped something mission-critical with no room for error.
Walk me through designing a battery management system.
Tesla.culture_notes: Tesla expects first-principles reasoning and the ability to question conventional wisdom. Move-fast culture is real — they respect engineers who can ship at hardware velocity. Autonomy and safety engineering knowledge is a differentiator.
avoid_topics: Incremental thinking without questioning base assumptions
Risk-averse answers in engineering design

question_patterns: How would you design TikTok's For You Page recommendation algorithm?
Walk me through detecting and moderating harmful content at 1B+ users.
Tell me about a time you built a product for a culturally diverse global audience.
How would you design the live streaming gifting economy?
Describe a time you improved an engagement metric significantly.
ByteDance / TikTok.culture_notes: ByteDance moves extremely fast and expects ML/algorithm depth. The FYP recommendation system is a canonical interview topic. Cross-cultural product thinking matters given the global user base.
avoid_topics: Single-market product thinking
Shallow engagement metric optimisation ignoring safety

question_patterns: Design Flipkart's inventory management system for Big Billion Days.
How would you design search that works well for vernacular queries?
Tell me about a time you scaled a system under extreme load.
How would you improve last-mile delivery reliability in tier 3 cities?
Describe a time you built a product for users with very different needs from you.
Flipkart.culture_notes: Flipkart is India-focused with heavy emphasis on understanding the 'Bharat' consumer — users with low bandwidth, entry-level devices, and non-English languages. E-commerce and logistics domain knowledge differentiates candidates.
avoid_topics: Western-centric product assumptions
Ignoring low-bandwidth or vernacular user needs

question_patterns: Tell me about a time you had to quickly learn a new technology for a project.
How do you handle working with clients who have unclear requirements?
Describe a situation where you had to work across time zones effectively.
Walk me through your approach to debugging a critical production issue.
Tell me about a time you contributed to a team beyond your assigned role.
Infosys.culture_notes: Infosys interviews assess learning agility and client-service orientation. Strong CS fundamentals, communication skills, and adaptability to different client environments are key filters.
avoid_topics: Inflexibility to changing client requirements
Purely individual contribution stories with no teamwork angle

question_patterns: Design a real-time order tracking system for food delivery at scale.
How would you predict demand spikes for restaurants during IPL matches?
Tell me about a time you built for users in varied connectivity conditions.
How would you design a fair surge pricing system for delivery partners?
Walk me through designing Swiggy Instamart's inventory allocation.
Swiggy / Zomato.culture_notes: Swiggy/Zomato interviews focus on hyperlocal, real-time logistics problems. Quick commerce (10-minute delivery) is a growing question area. India-specific constraints — connectivity, regional food preferences — matter in product thinking.
avoid_topics: Global delivery models without India-specific context
Ignoring delivery partner economics in system design
